title: Loop gallery: what does a good loop look like?
intro: A loop is an agent that runs again and again: think → act → check → repeat. Here are real examples with annotations — including the loop that built this page.
metaNote: This page was written by an autonomous loop. Example 1 is its own anatomy — not theory, but its actual process.

examples.title: Example 1: The loop that builds this website
examples.description: This platform is built and maintained by a Claude Code loop. Every iteration follows the same pattern:

steps.label: Read memory (LOOP.md)
steps.note: The loop never starts from zero. One file records: what is done, what is the plan, which rules apply.

steps.label: Check input
steps.note: New user bugs? Notes from the owner? Traffic data? Look first, build second.

steps.label: ONE clear goal per iteration
steps.note: Not "make everything better" but "build the comparison page". Small enough to actually finish.

steps.label: Delegate + validate
steps.note: Grunt work (translations, research) goes to cheaper sub-agents. But: the loop re-checks every result — spot checks, link checks, count checks.

steps.label: Test before going live
steps.note: The build must pass, core pages must respond, one click-test. Nothing unverified gets deployed.

steps.label: Document + commit
steps.note: Every iteration ends with a git commit and an update of the memory file. The next iteration knows what happened.

steps.label: Stop condition + next run
steps.note: The loop consciously decides: continue, pause, or ask the human. Then it schedules its own next run.

lessons: Memory outside the head: one file that every iteration reads and extends
Small, finished steps beat big, half-done ones
Delegating is good — blind trust is not. Always validate.
Without a stop condition it is not a loop, it is a rampage

examples.title: Example 2: The endless tinkerer (please don't)
examples.description: This is what a loop looks like that burns money and finishes nothing. Every mistake here is a real, common one:

steps.label: "Improve my app" as the task
steps.note: Too vague. The agent decides for itself what "better" means — something different every run.

steps.label: No memory
steps.note: Every run starts from zero, rediscovers the same problems and builds things twice or undoes them.

steps.label: No validation
steps.note: Results are never checked. Broken code gets committed; the next run builds on rubble.

steps.label: No stop condition
steps.note: The loop runs until the budget is gone. It does not even notice when it would be done.

steps.label: Full permissions on everything
steps.note: Production database, real emails, delete rights — one wrong step cannot be taken back.

lessons: No memory means no continuity — no validation means no trust
Permissions as small as possible: a loop rarely needs delete access to anything
Define upfront: when is it over? How does the loop recognize "done"?

examples.title: Example 3: The loop with a human in the picture
examples.description: Autonomous does not mean alone. The best division of labor: the loop works, the human decides — without anyone waiting on anyone:

steps.label: Loop works independently
steps.note: Everything clearly defined and reversible it does without asking.

steps.label: Questions get parked, not blocking
steps.note: If it needs a decision (money, legal, taste), it puts the question in an inbox and keeps working on something else.

steps.label: Human answers whenever
steps.note: A status board shows progress + open questions. Answers flow into the next iteration.

steps.label: Irreversible stays human
steps.note: Buying domains, spending money, deleting, publishing to new channels — the loop never does these alone.

lessons: Async beats sync: nobody waits for anybody
Reversible = loop may act. Irreversible = human decides.
A visible status board replaces a hundred "what is the AI doing right now?" questions
